epigraph.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies 
proclaim the work of his hands.

content.text:
Ratios and proportions have applications in virtually every field. In 
this section, we explore some of the most common and useful applications.

When two quantities vary directly, as one increases, the other increases 
proportionally.

content.title: Direct Variation
content.content:
Two quantities $x$ and $y$ are in **direct variation** (or vary 
directly) if there is a constant $k$ such that:
\[
y = kx \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{y}{x} = k
\]
The constant $k$ is called the **constant of variation**.

content.title: Direct variation
content.problem:
The distance traveled varies directly with time when speed is constant. 
If a car travels 120 miles in 2 hours, how far will it travel in 5 hours?
content.solution:
Find the constant of variation: $k = \frac{120 \text{ mi}}{2 \text{ hr}} = 60$ mph.

For 5 hours: $d = 60 \times 5 = 300$ miles.

Or use a proportion: $\frac{120}{2} = \frac{d}{5}$, giving $d = 300$.

The car will travel $\boxed{300 \text{ miles}}$.

content.text:
In inverse variation, as one quantity increases, the other decreases 
proportionally.

content.title: Inverse Variation
content.content:
Two quantities $x$ and $y$ are in **inverse variation** if there 
is a constant $k$ such that:
\[
xy = k \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y = \frac{k}{x}
\]

content.title: Inverse variation
content.problem: If 4 workers can complete a job in 6 days, how long will it take 8 workers?
content.solution:
This is inverse variation: more workers means less time.

$\text{workers} \times \text{days} = k$

$4 \times 6 = 24 = k$

For 8 workers: $8 \times d = 24$, so $d = 3$.

It will take $\boxed{3 \text{ days}}$.

content.text: Mixing problems often involve maintaining or achieving certain ratios.

content.title: Mixing solutions
content.problem:
A painter mixes blue and yellow paint in a ratio of 2:5 to create green. 
If he uses 8 cups of blue paint, how much yellow paint does he need?
content.solution:
Set up a proportion:
\[
\frac{\text{blue}}{\text{yellow}} = \frac{2}{5} = \frac{8}{y}
\]

Cross multiply: $2y = 40$, so $y = 20$.

He needs $\boxed{20 \text{ cups}}$ of yellow paint.

content.title: Concentration problem
content.problem:
A solution is 15\% salt. If you have 200 grams of solution, how many 
grams of salt does it contain?
content.solution:
15\% means $\frac{15}{100}$ of the solution is salt:
\[
\text{salt} = 0.15 \times 200 = 30 \text{ grams}
\]

The solution contains $\boxed{30 \text{ grams}}$ of salt.

content.text:
Rates of change describe how quickly one quantity changes relative to 
another.

content.title: Population growth rate
content.problem:
A city's population grew from 50,000 to 62,500 in 5 years. What was the 
average annual growth?
content.solution:
Total change: $62,500 - 50,000 = 12,500$ people.

Rate of change: $\frac{12,500 \text{ people}}{5 \text{ years}} = 2,500$ people per year.

The average annual growth was $\boxed{2,500 \text{ people per year}}$.

content.title: Growth of the early church
content.problem:
According to Acts, the early church grew from about 120 (Acts 1:15) to 
about 3,000 after Pentecost (Acts 2:41). By what factor did it grow?
content.solution:
Growth factor: $\frac{3000}{120} = 25$

The church grew by a factor of $\boxed{25}$, or 2,500\%.

content.text: Scientists use ratios and proportions constantly.

content.title: Gear ratios
content.problem:
A bicycle's gear ratio is 3:1 (the pedal gear has 3 times as many teeth 
as the wheel gear). If the pedal gear rotates 20 times, how many times 
does the wheel gear rotate?
content.solution:
With a 3:1 ratio, the wheel gear rotates 3 times for each rotation of 
the pedal gear:
\[
20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ rotations}
\]

The wheel gear rotates $\boxed{60 \text{ times}}$.

content.title: Scale model
content.problem:
A model of the solar system uses the scale 1 cm : 10 million km. Earth 
is about 150 million km from the Sun. How far from the Sun should Earth 
be placed in the model?
content.solution:
\[
\frac{1 \text{ cm}}{10 \text{ million km}} = \frac{x \text{ cm}}{150 \text{ million km}}
\]

$x = \frac{150}{10} = 15$ cm.

Earth should be $\boxed{15 \text{ cm}}$ from the Sun.

content.text: Proportional reasoning is essential in finance.

content.title: Currency exchange
content.problem: If \$1 USD = 0.92 euros, how many euros do you get for \$350?
content.solution:
\[
\frac{\$1}{0.92 \text{ euros}} = \frac{\$350}{x \text{ euros}}
\]

$x = 350 \times 0.92 = 322$ euros.

You get $\boxed{322 \text{ euros}}$.

content.title: Profit margin
content.problem:
A store has a profit margin of 30\%. If an item costs the store \$42, 
what should the selling price be?
content.solution:
If profit is 30\% of the selling price, then cost is 70\%:
\[
0.70 \times \text{selling price} = \$42
\]
$\text{selling price} = \frac{42}{0.70} = \$60$

The selling price should be ${\$60}$.

marginNotes.text: A man reaps what he sows.

With it prepare a grain offering every 
morning—a sixth of an ephah, with a third of a hin of oil to moisten 
the flour.

Who marked off its dimensions? Surely you 
know! Who stretched a measuring line across it?

Why then didn't you put my money on deposit, 
so that when I came back, I could have collected it with interest?

marginNotes.author: Bertrand Russell
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics takes us into the region of 
absolute necessity, to which not only the actual world, but every 
possible world, must conform.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
Truth is ever to be found in simplicity, and 
not in the multiplicity and confusion of things.
